title: ci: move some jobs from Cirrus to GitHub Actions
body:
Move some cirrus jobs to gha. High-level overview:
- ~~ci: rm contrib/cirrus/check_go_changes.sh~~
replaced with https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/28753
- ci: rm contrib/cirrus/pr-should-link-jira
- ci: move pr-removes-fixed-skips from Cirrus to GitHub Actions
- ci: move pr-should-include-tests from Cirrus to GitHub Actions
- ci: move leading-tabs system-test check from Cirrus to GitHub Actions
- ci: move shellcheck from Cirrus to GitHub Actions
- ci: move renovate config validation from Cirrus to GitHub Actions
- ci: move tests-expect-exit check from Makefile/Cirrus to GitHub Actions
- ci: move swagger-check from Cirrus to GitHub Actions
- ~~ci: replace .gitvalidation with inline commit-subject-length check~~
- ~~test/tools: drop git-validation~~
replaced with https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/28752
- ci: add all-done aggregator job to validate workflow

#### Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?

```release-note
NONE
```

comments.body: [NON-BLOCKING] Packit jobs failed. @containers/packit-build please check. Everyone else, feel free to ignore.

OK I think this is good enough to be merged as a separate PR, and it's ready for review.

@Luap99 @timcoding1988 @ashley-cui I'm going to tackle golangci-lint in the next PR (to conclude the validate-source migration), let me know if someone is already working on it.

> I do not really like having a ton of different validation jobs, this will be a pain to setup for merge protection and job dependecies and so on.

I concur; that's why there's `all-done` job which combines all the others, and the protection rule is simplified to requiring "all-done". With the current setup, there will be a multitude of all-done jobs but if we put everything in one file we can probably have just one.

> Also we cannot setup merge protection until the final move so this cannot/should not be merged before either.
> 
> Overall we need to keep our current structure as much as possible, i.e. liokely m ost of this needs to end up[ in one huge workflow yml file so we can have proper job dependencies. Because as we have now if the build/validation fails we must not start the the real test jobs

Gotcha.

> I do not really like having a ton of different validation jobs, this will be a pain to setup for merge protection and job dependecies and so on. > Also we cannot setup merge protection until the final move so this cannot/should not be merged before either. I respectfully disagree with both points 1. The only extra step needed to simplify the branch protection rules is to add a job which lists all other jobs as dependencies. This is what's done by the last commit here (look for `all-done`). Once it is set up, the branch protection rules just list **a single** all-done (doesn't matter which job/task it comes from). For example, we have 56 jobs in runc, and this is how the branch protection rules look like: 2. For the same reason, we can set up branch protection at any point and gradually migrate and add new jobs (as long as they are listed in `all-done`, we don't have to change anything. Surely, we'll need to remove the jobs we're removing. Having said that, I might be missing something here as I don't see the current branch protection rules for this repo.

> The only extra step needed to simplify the branch protection rules is to add a job which lists all other jobs as dependencies. This is what's done by the last commit here (look for all-done). Once it is set up, the branch protection rules just list a single all-done (doesn't matter which job/task it comes from). For example, we have 56 jobs in runc, and this is how the branch protection rules look like:

Yes we have a final "total success" task in the end, still you need to somehow manage all jobs as explicit deps. When we fully move to github actions that is fine and will need to happen no matter what but then it all needs to be in one big file so we can actually not start the "real tests" when any validation job fails.

What I mean is we cannot merge this without doing the full proper migration all at once, yes I could add all-done to merge protections technically right now but then all the slow/expensive "real tests" still run when this github action fails. That is what I mean with complex job dependencies.

Personally I am not a fan of a ton of different small gh action jobs for validation compared to one lager one (that is preference of course) mainly because cncf wants us to use their runners and they are bigger and take like 1 minute to start tasks and it feels kinda wasted to spawn many CI jobs that run a few seconds in such a system. The overhead of setting up the job will then be much slower than the actual job wasting resources IMO.

Maintenance wise with small jobs we have the problem of having to ensure that a local `make validate` is doing the exact same checks that all github actions jobs are doing. To me it feels much more natural to use the same make target in CI and local to ensure there is no drift.

Mhh I just see some of these formatting checks where not part of the local make target at all so this would likely be good to fix first IMO, i.e. the system test formatting check or tests-expect-exit is stuff that can and should be done locally so contributors do not get hit with basic validation failures in CI that we could have caught locally

For the should include tests and skip fixmes that of course depends on CI so it makes sense to use the extra gh job for that here

Thanks, I was (and am) going to try to make jobs work locally as well as much as possible; will also try to use 1 big job rather than many small ones.

I still like the small jobs as
 - they start fast (using GHA runners) and run in parallel, so you can see the results in a minute or two after opening a PR
 - you don't have to look deeper to see what's wrong (for example, if shellcheck or golangci-lint job fails, you know you need to run shellcheck/golangci-lint
 
The biggest con of small jobs is, of course, the repeated setup steps, and the need to aggregate the status (into something like all-done).

Surely in case of using CNCF runners that are big and slower to start, it makes more sense to go with longer-running fewer jobs, so I'm going to try implementing validate-source as one job.

Yeah I mean you are not wrong, but with the self hosted runners it does not seem ideal right now.

For now this week we will need to just have something working, it will not be perfect and I happily rework things later. We will need to see how much of the default github provided runners we can use. I think the main point CNCF wise is we cannot (or should not, no idea if it is enforced) exceed the free limits. And because CNCF does not provide their own windows/macos runners we have to use the gh ones there and see how much this will cut into the free quota, the windows machine tests are not fast. If we have enough free capacity I think we can use the github runners for the small quick validation tasks and split them where it makes sense.
